title: Mitigating Judge Bias
concept: Mitigate bias by: swapping response order (evaluating both A-B and B-A), using detailed grading rubrics, and restricting response length.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is true about LLM judges?

options.text: They can have biases, but we can use techniques to mitigate them.
They are always 100% fair and never need rules.
They only prefer the shortest answers available.

exercises.explain: Just like humans, AI judges have biases (like preferring the first response or longer answers). We must design methods to reduce these biases.

cards.title: Keeping it Fair
cards.body: AI judges have favorite positions and love long, wordy answers, even if they are wrong! How do we stop this bias and get fair results? Let's learn how to make our AI judges neutral.

cards.title: Mitigating Judge Bias
cards.body: We can mitigate (reduce) judge bias by using three smart habits: swapping the order of the answers we show the judge, using strict grading rubrics, and making sure the answers are about the same length.

cards.title: The Cake Tasting Order
cards.body: Imagine you are judging a cake contest. If you always taste the chocolate cake first, you might be tired or full by the time you taste the vanilla cake. To be fair, you should have some judges taste chocolate first, and others taste vanilla first! Swapping the order keeps the judgment fair.

cards.title: Mitigating in Action
cards.body: Instead of just asking an LLM: 'Which answer is better, A or B?', you change the prompt to: 'Evaluate these based on accuracy, clarity, and tone. Now, swap the order and check if B or A is better. Keep both answers under 100 words.'

cards.title: Are LLMs Naturally Fair?
cards.body: You might think an LLM judge is naturally objective like a real judge in court. In reality, LLM judges are easily tricked by which answer they read first (position bias) or how fancy and long an answer looks (verbosity bias). We must actively set rules to stop this!

cards.title: Biased vs. Mitigated Evaluation
cards.body: See the difference between letting a judge run wild versus setting up strict safety controls to prevent bias.

left.text: Evaluates one order only, loves long paragraphs, grades on vibes alone.

right.text: Evaluates A-B and B-A, uses a rubric, compares equal length texts.

exercises.prompt: How can we mitigate "position bias" where a judge prefers the first response it sees?

options.text: By making the first response much longer.
By swapping the order (evaluating both A-B and B-A).
By asking the judge to ignore the second response.
By using emojis in only one response.

exercises.explain: Position bias means the judge favors the first option it reads. Swapping the presentation order to evaluate both A-B and B-A helps cancel this bias out.

exercises.prompt: To mitigate position bias, you should present the choices to the judge in both orders (A-B and B-A).
exercises.explain: Evaluating both A-B and B-A presentation orders is crucial because AI judges often favor the first response they see.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the strategy for reducing bias:
exercises.sentence: To stop a judge from using vibes, we should grade using a detailed rubric.
exercises.explain: A detailed grading rubric gives the judge specific criteria to look for, rather than letting it decide based on vibes or response length.

exercises.prompt: Why is restricting response length an effective way to mitigate judge bias?

options.text: The judge will get bored and stop reading.
It makes the judge pick the first option every time.
It stops the judge from favoring longer answers just because they look impressive.

exercises.explain: Restricting length prevents the judge from choosing an answer simply because it is longer and looks more detailed (verbosity bias).

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to compare two AI models using an LLM judge. Which combination of strategies will best prevent judge bias?

options.text: Only test one order, write a short prompt, and let the judge guess the length.
Swap the prompt order (A-B and B-A), use a strict rubric, and control response lengths.
Ask the judge to write its own rules and always put your favorite model first.
Make one response five times longer than the other to see if the judge notices.

exercises.explain: By evaluating both A-B and B-A order, using a structured grading rubric, and keeping length equal, you prevent the LLM judge from picking answers based on position, length, or vague feelings.
